topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up, a stark intrusion on the otherwise quiet afternoon. My heart gave a sudden, unwelcome lurch. It was a direct message from Mr. Harrison. I froze, my fingers hovering over the keyboard. He’d been a constant presence in my life, the stern but brilliant history teacher. Now, this digital tether felt like a tripwire. I quickly minimized the window, my breath catching in my throat. A cold sweat prickled my palms as I stared at the profile picture—the same slightly aloof, serious expression he wore in class—now overlaid with a subtle, knowing smirk. Every muscle in my jaw tightened. I had to pretend I hadn't seen it, to act as if the screen was blank. The silence of my room amplified the frantic drumming in my chest.

[story 2]

I was meticulously organizing my notes, a futile attempt to anchor myself in the familiar routine of academia, when the alert flashed. It was a follow request from Professor Albright. A wave of nausea washed over me, making the edges of my vision swim slightly. I dropped my pen; it clattered loudly on the wooden desk. My hands started to shake, a fine tremor running up my arms. I glanced at the screen, my gaze lingering on the unfamiliar icon. Why? What was he looking for? I felt a desperate urge to slam my laptop shut, to erase the evidence of this unexpected connection. A strange heat bloomed in my cheeks, and I could feel the weight of his unseen observation pressing down on me, suffocating the air in the small study.

[story 3]

It happened during lunch break. I was trying to enjoy a solitary sandwich, but my attention kept drifting to my phone. A notification—a tag on a recent post from Mr. Harrison, a post that seemed innocuous enough, yet it felt loaded. My stomach clenched, a tight knot forming just beneath my ribs. I deliberately turned my phone face down, pressing it against my thigh, trying to smother the urge to check again. The ambient noise of the cafeteria seemed to fade away, replaced by the loud, rushing sound of my own pulse. I kept my eyes fixed on my tray, forcing my breathing to slow, trying to regulate the rapid flutter inside me as I waited for the inevitable digital echo.
